claim_verification.claim: DDC resilience comes from fragmenting data so no single node holds a complete copy, and single-node failure/removal does not interrupt access.
claim_verification.notes: Directly supported by multiple statements about fragmentation, no single node holding full copy, and continued access on node failure.

claim_verification.claim: Edge clusters can operate autonomously (storage, routing, compute, request-level authentication) without live blockchain or external coordination services.
claim_verification.notes: Matches the explicit definition of an autonomous edge cluster.

claim_verification.claim: DDC uses erasure coding for fragmentation and can reconstruct data when a subset of nodes is unavailable.
claim_verification.notes: Explicitly stated: fragmentation implemented through erasure coding enabling reconstruction with missing nodes.

claim_verification.claim: Dragon 1 uses a 16/48 erasure coding scheme; any 16 of 48 chunks reconstruct the data; tolerates loss of up to 32 nodes without data loss.
claim_verification.notes: 16/48 scheme is in ADR; 32-node loss tolerance is explicitly stated in Core Wiki.

claim_verification.claim: DDC clusters automatically redirect client requests to remaining active nodes when nodes go offline, ensuring uninterrupted access.
claim_verification.notes: Explicitly stated: continuous request routing and automatic redirection supported by erasure coding.

claim_verification.claim: The DDC Orchestrator detects node failure and initiates workload redistribution without manual intervention.
claim_verification.notes: Directly supported by the Orchestrator redistribution description.

claim_verification.claim: DDC clusters operate without a centralized coordinator, relying on distributed protocols for routing/data placement/replication decisions.
claim_verification.notes: This is explicitly stated in the provided evidence (even though the rubric notes it as aspirational/usually implied).

claim_verification.claim: DDC uses Kademlia DHT for topology management, enabling nodes to independently resolve routing paths and keep data plane operations available during blockchain/control-plane interruption.
claim_verification.notes: Supported by the Kademlia DHT paragraph.

claim_verification.claim: Storage/cache nodes maintain bucket and customer information locally, enabling continued operation during blockchain unavailability.
claim_verification.notes: Explicitly stated.

claim_verification.claim: Storage/cache nodes maintain a nodes index persisted on disk enabling continued operation when blockchain is unavailable.
claim_verification.notes: Supported by the disk-persisted indexes statement.

claim_verification.claim: The blockchain is an immutable repository for validation results and cluster configuration rather than a real-time gatekeeper.
claim_verification.notes: Explicitly stated.

claim_verification.claim: Nodes authenticate requests by verifying cryptographic signatures; SDK derives signing key locally from seed/wallet; nodes never see seed material.
claim_verification.notes: Supported by the authentication description and blockchain-independence section.

claim_verification.claim: This enables the storage layer to maintain availability and Byzantine fault tolerance independently of blockchain connectivity during transient outages.
claim_verification.notes: BFT independence from chain connectivity is explicitly stated.

claim_verification.claim: Edge/hybrid configurations target 99.9% or higher availability.
claim_verification.notes: Explicitly stated as a target; not an SLA.

claim_verification.claim: Client request latency didn't change at all after several nodes were turned off (after retrieval logic update).
claim_verification.notes: Direct quote in evidence.

claim_verification.claim: DDC edge clusters require strict majority quorum; 5-node cluster needs 3 nodes for read/write; below quorum clusters suspend operations to prevent split-brain.
claim_verification.notes: Explicitly stated in evidence.

claim_verification.claim: Edge clusters support regional data sovereignty by keeping processing/storage within geographic boundaries and operating independently so data does not traverse external nodes outside the region.
claim_verification.notes: Explicitly stated.

claim_verification.claim: When a network partition separates edge-local nodes in a hybrid cluster, edge nodes continue serving local operations (availability-favoring CAP trade-off).
claim_verification.notes: This behavior is explicitly described in the evidence.

claim_verification.claim: Upon reconnection, DDC reconciles state using CRDT semantics, merging concurrent writes deterministically and ensuring zero data loss by construction.
claim_verification.notes: Explicitly stated in evidence.

claim_verification.claim: DDC initiates data repair when nodes are permanently lost; simulation shows repair started and data size grew on other nodes.
claim_verification.notes: Directly supported by simulation log excerpt.

claim_verification.claim: For edge deployments, repair processes prioritize critical data segments first and defer lower-priority fragments until bandwidth is available.
claim_verification.notes: Explicitly stated in evidence.

claim_verification.claim: DDC is suitable for enterprises because each DDC cluster is a complete, independent cloud replacing VPC/S3/compute with on-chain verification.
claim_verification.notes: Directly supported.

claim_verification.claim: DDC uses replication for small pieces and erasure coding for pieces larger than 16KB.
claim_verification.notes: Evidence says 'Small pieces: Replication' and 'Pieces larger than 16 KB: Erasure coding' but the answer did not cite this source in the relevant section; it instead cites DDC Core Wiki p.1. This is an UNSUPPORTED claim under the rubric because it lacks a correct citation.

claim_verification.claim: DDC clusters automatically redirect requests and Orchestrator redistributes workloads; therefore 'no interruption' and 'without requiring manual intervention' always holds.
claim_verification.notes: Evidence supports automatic redirection and redistribution, but the answer sometimes generalizes to uninterrupted operation without qualifying quorum loss cases (edge clusters can suspend operations below quorum).

claim_verification.claim: DDC nodes verify cryptographic signatures tied to requesting identity; SDK derives signing key from seed phrase or wallet; nodes never have access to seed material.
claim_verification.notes: Supported.

claim_verification.claim: DDC provides 'Byzantine fault tolerance principles' and 'split-brain prevention' as part of quorum design.
claim_verification.notes: The quorum paragraph explicitly references BFT and split-brain prevention.

claim_verification.claim: DDC uses 'disk-persisted indexes for blockchain-independent operation' including restart behavior under chain outage.
claim_verification.notes: The answer cites p.34 for nodes index; the stronger restart-specific language is supported in p.033/p.034 evidence (nodes index and auth gater indexes).

claim_verification.claim: DDC adopts Kademlia DHT replacing earlier Hash Ring-based solution.
claim_verification.notes: Explicitly stated.

claim_verification.claim: Hybrid cluster partition handling uses CRDT reconciliation and last-write-wins fallback with logical timestamps.
claim_verification.notes: The evidence includes the LWW fallback and logical timestamps details.

claim_verification.claim: Protocol-level Sybil resistance remains on the roadmap.
claim_verification.notes: Explicitly stated.

claim_verification.claim: SDK-integrated encryption is in development; encryption at rest is not enabled by default and data is plaintext unless client encrypts.
claim_verification.notes: Both points are supported.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: DHT-based peer discovery and self-bootstrap (nodes can use themselves as bootstrap)
required_concepts_coverage.notes: Mentions Kademlia DHT and coordinator-free routing, but does not cover the specific self-bootstrap behavior ('use self as bootstrap if no nodes found') from DDC Core Wiki-p005/p006.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: Node failure tolerance via erasure coding (K/N scheme, Dragon 1: 16/48)
required_concepts_coverage.notes: Covered in section 2 with 16/48 and 32-node loss tolerance.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: Data replication for small pieces, erasure coding for large pieces
required_concepts_coverage.notes: The answer does not correctly ground this concept with evidence/citation; it asserts erasure coding broadly and gives 16/48, but does not properly cite the small-piece replication vs >16KB erasure coding split.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: Automatic data repair when nodes join or leave the cluster
required_concepts_coverage.notes: Cites repair triggered and data size grew on other nodes; also discusses repair mechanisms.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: Adaptive routing around slow/failed nodes
required_concepts_coverage.notes: Cites retrieval logic update launching additional requests to other nodes and latency unchanged after outages.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: Disk-persisted indexes for blockchain-independent operation
required_concepts_coverage.notes: Discusses local bucket/customer info and disk-persisted nodes index enabling operation during chain outages.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: Each cluster as a complete, independent cloud
required_concepts_coverage.notes: Cites DDC from Sergey Poluyan describing complete independent cloud.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: Regional optimization (clusters for specific data types and regions)
required_concepts_coverage.notes: Cites regional optimization examples (NZ low-latency transactional, Central Asia streaming) and data sovereignty.

forbidden_claims_found: Specific minimum node counts as current production fact (answer implies minimum 5 nodes as a general requirement; evidence frames 5 nodes as recommended/minimum in topology description but production status is not clearly established).
outside_knowledge_detected: Use of 'CAP theorem trade-offs' framing (the evidence uses this phrase, so not outside knowledge by itself), but the answer adds interpretive language about 'availability-favoring behavior consistent with CAP theorem trade-offs' beyond what is needed; still largely aligned with evidence.
failures: At least one required documented concept ('replication for small pieces, erasure coding for large pieces') is not properly supported with correct citation; the claim is present but uncited/miscited, reducing grounded correctness and completeness.
Overstatement risk: claims of uninterrupted operation are not consistently qualified by quorum-loss behavior (edge clusters can suspend operations below quorum).
missing_topics: Self-bootstrap details for DHT peer discovery (use self as bootstrap if no nodes found).
Explicit small-piece replication vs large-piece erasure coding split with correct citation.

suggested_doc_improvements.description: Add a concise, single section that states the redundancy policy split (replication for small pieces vs erasure coding for larger pieces) in the Core Wiki resilience overview, with thresholds and current defaults.
suggested_doc_improvements.reason: This is a required concept for explaining resilience, but it is currently easier to miss or mis-cite because it appears outside the main Core Wiki resilience summary.

suggested_doc_improvements.description: Document DHT bootstrap behavior (including 'use self as bootstrap if no peers found') in the main architecture/resilience section, not only in delivery-plan/spec fragments.
suggested_doc_improvements.reason: This is a key mechanism for autonomous/edge suitability and peer-to-peer resilience, and it is required by the rubric.

suggested_doc_improvements.description: Clarify in topology docs whether 'minimum 5 nodes' is a recommendation, a hard minimum, or a profile-dependent constraint, and whether it applies to production deployments.
suggested_doc_improvements.reason: Prevents answers from overstating node-count requirements as production facts.

suggested_doc_improvements.description: Add a concise, canonical 'Security vs Traditional Cloud' section that explicitly distinguishes: (a) what is enforced by protocol today (signatures, auth tokens, bucket permissions), (b) what is optional/manual (client-side encryption, TLS configuration), and (c) what is planned (SDK-integrated encryption/key delegation).
suggested_doc_improvements.reason: Prevents common overclaims like 'encryption is required' and clarifies conditional security properties.

suggested_doc_improvements.description: Document the multi-provider deployment assumption (e.g., 'at least 5 cloud providers' for testnet/mainnet infra) in the security comparison section, including what threat it mitigates (provider outage concentration) and what it does not (confidentiality).
suggested_doc_improvements.reason: This is a required concept in the rubric and is currently easy to miss in security-focused answers.

suggested_doc_improvements.description: Clarify the exact scope of 'verified' DAC outputs: what is written on-chain (validation results/config state) vs what remains off-chain logs/records, and avoid ambiguous language that could be interpreted as a full cryptographic audit trail of all activity.
suggested_doc_improvements.reason: Reduces over-interpretation and aligns claims with evidence language ('verified data source' vs 'cryptographically verified audit trail').

claim_verification.claim: To store data on a DDC cluster, you generate an sr25519 data wallet keypair, fund it with CERE tokens and a DDC deposit, create a bucket, and upload data using the DDC SDK or CLI.
claim_verification.notes: CLI account creation shows sr25519 and address; SDK init, bucket creation, and store() are documented; deposit + positive balance prerequisites are documented.

claim_verification.claim: Data is stored in plaintext by default; security relies on client-side encryption (which you must implement yourself).
evidence_chunks: Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p001
Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p004
Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p043
claim_verification.notes: Multiple chunks explicitly state plaintext by default and that client must encrypt before upload.

claim_verification.claim: Security relies on data fragmentation via erasure coding across cluster nodes.
claim_verification.notes: Docs state fragmentation/erasure coding is used for fault tolerance and that nodes see fragments; however, calling it a 'security' mechanism is potentially misleading (see precision).

claim_verification.claim: A data wallet is an sr25519 cryptographic keypair that serves as your identity within the DDC network and consists of public key, private key, and mnemonic seed phrase.
evidence_chunks: Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p040
Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p002
claim_verification.notes: This definition is explicitly present in DDC Client JS SDK Wiki p004 and reiterated in Encrypted Data Access doc.

claim_verification.claim: You generate a new data wallet using `npx @cere-ddc-sdk/cli account --random` and must store the mnemonic securely.
claim_verification.notes: Command and warning are directly documented.

claim_verification.claim: Before initializing a DDC client instance, the account must have a positive CERE token balance and a DDC-specific deposit funded prior to creating a client instance.
claim_verification.notes: Exact requirement is stated in the SDK wiki.

claim_verification.claim: To make the deposit programmatically: `blockchain.ddcCustomers.deposit(deposit); await blockchain.send(tx, { account })`.
claim_verification.notes: Code example is present.

claim_verification.claim: Initialize the DDC client with your seed phrase: `const client = await DdcClient.create(seedPhrase, TESTNET);`.
claim_verification.notes: Matches documented examples.

claim_verification.claim: Create a bucket with `client.createBucket(clusterId, { isPublic: true })`.
claim_verification.notes: SDK wiki shows createBucket with isPublic option.

claim_verification.claim: When a bucket is created, the wallet that initiated the transaction automatically becomes the bucket owner.
evidence_chunks: Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p002
claim_verification.notes: ADR states wallet that created bucket becomes bucket owner with root access; core wiki also states only bucket owners can access or grant access.

claim_verification.claim: Upload a file using `client.store(bucketId, file)` and receive a CID.
claim_verification.notes: Both docs show store() returning cid/fileUri.cid.

claim_verification.claim: CID is based on the content's cryptographic hash label used to point to material.
claim_verification.notes: Definition is explicit.

claim_verification.claim: The SDK authenticates by signing requests with a user key derived from seed/wallet; nodes verify the signature; authorization is enforced based on bucket permissions and on-chain state.
claim_verification.notes: SDK wiki covers signing and authorization enforcement; core wiki covers request-level signing and node verification without seed exposure.

claim_verification.claim: The signature is constructed using sr25519 over requestId, bucketId, timestamp, and userPublicKey.
claim_verification.notes: Exact signatureBytes formula is documented.

claim_verification.claim: DDC uses recursive JWT-like signed tokens for access delegation and tokens rely on expiration times.
claim_verification.notes: ADR describes recursive JWT-like trust chain and includes expiresAt in token payload.

claim_verification.claim: DDC does not currently enforce TLS on client-to-node connections by default; data is transmitted in plaintext between clients and storage nodes.
evidence_chunks: Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p002
claim_verification.notes: Both sources explicitly state TLS not enforced by default and plaintext in transit.

claim_verification.claim: To encrypt before upload, generate a DEK and use AES-256-GCM to encrypt locally, then upload ciphertext.
evidence_chunks: Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p001
Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p009
claim_verification.notes: Docs explicitly recommend AES-256-GCM and show example steps.

claim_verification.claim: The DEK is generated and held exclusively by the client and is never transmitted to or stored by any node; no administrative override or cluster-level key recovery exists; loss of DEK makes data inaccessible.
evidence_chunks: Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p001
Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p005
claim_verification.notes: All points are explicitly stated.

claim_verification.claim: The Dragon 1 cluster uses a 16/48 erasure coding scheme.
claim_verification.notes: Data redundancy ADR states Dragon 1 has 16/48.

claim_verification.claim: Nodes verify that the associated bucket holds a sufficient balance to cover the operation and may retain authorization details for activity capture.
claim_verification.notes: Both are explicitly described.

claim_verification.claim: DDC storage and cache nodes continue serving requests even when blockchain is temporarily unavailable; nodes maintain bucket and customer indexes on disk.
claim_verification.notes: Both statements appear in core wiki.

claim_verification.claim: Loss of the wallet key/mnemonic results in permanent, unrecoverable loss of access to all associated data.
evidence_chunks: Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p001
claim_verification.notes: Docs warn mnemonic loss prevents recovery; Encrypted Data Access warns permanent loss from lost credentials.

claim_verification.claim: The Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation ADR proposes HKDF key derivation, Key Escrow Service, and agent-specific key delegation.
evidence_chunks: Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p009
Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p028
Encrypted Data Access and Key Delegation-p047
claim_verification.notes: These are explicitly in the planned ADR.

claim_verification.claim: DDC has not yet published results from independent third-party security audits.
claim_verification.notes: Core wiki explicitly states audit results not published.

claim_verification.claim: Data is stored in plaintext by default — security relies on bucket-level access control enforced on-chain and data fragmentation via erasure coding.
claim_verification.notes: Access control and erasure coding are documented, but framing erasure coding as a 'security' basis is imprecise because docs explicitly say erasure coding is not a confidentiality control and node operators can read fragments.

required_concepts_coverage.notes: Section 'Creating Your Data Wallet' includes CLI command and mnemonic handling.

required_concepts_coverage.notes: Shows client.store() and describes CID returned.

required_concepts_coverage.notes: Explicitly defines CID as based on cryptographic hash.

required_concepts_coverage.notes: Explains SDK signs requests with key derived from seed/wallet; nodes verify.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: Authorization via on-chain bucket permissions
required_concepts_coverage.notes: States authorization enforced based on bucket permissions and on-chain state.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: Recursive token-based access delegation
required_concepts_coverage.notes: Explains recursive JWT-like token chain and expiration.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: Client-side encryption recommended for sensitive data
required_concepts_coverage.notes: Explicitly states plaintext default and recommends client-side encryption; provides DEK/AES-256-GCM guidance.

required_concepts_coverage.concept: Key loss consequences (mnemonic loss = account loss, DEK loss = data inaccessible)
required_concepts_coverage.notes: Has a 'Key Loss Warning' section covering mnemonic and DEK loss consequences.

outside_knowledge_detected: Mentions 'AES-256-GCM' as 'computationally resistant to brute-force attacks under current conditions' (this specific security assurance is not stated in evidence; docs only recommend AES-256-GCM).
failures: Correctness below pass threshold (0.78 < 0.8) due to imprecise/overstated security framing (erasure coding presented as part of 'what makes it secure') and at least one outside-knowledge security assurance about AES-256-GCM.

suggested_doc_improvements.description: Add a short 'Security properties vs non-properties' section explicitly stating that erasure coding improves availability/durability but does not provide confidentiality, and clarify how it should/should not be described as 'security'.
suggested_doc_improvements.reason: Prevents users from misinterpreting fragmentation/erasure coding as a confidentiality guarantee.

suggested_doc_improvements.description: Add an explicit, consolidated 'Plaintext by default' warning to the Get Started guide near the upload step, with a link to the encryption-before-upload guidance.
suggested_doc_improvements.reason: Reduces risk that new users store sensitive data unencrypted by following the quickstart.

suggested_doc_improvements.description: Document a minimal end-to-end example that includes optional encryption (DEK generation, encrypt, store, read, decrypt) alongside the existing store/read examples.
suggested_doc_improvements.reason: Improves practical adoption of the recommended client-side encryption flow without requiring readers to stitch multiple documents together.
